mn11:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn11:10.1: There are, *bhikkhus*, some ascetics and brahmins who claim to speak of the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññāvādā*].
mn11:10.2: But they do not correctly explain the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññaṁ*]—
mn11:10.3: they explain the full understanding of clinging to sensual pleasures [*kāmupādānassa*], but they do not explain the full understanding of clinging to views [*diṭṭhupādānassa*], they do not explain the full understanding of clinging to precepts and observances [*sīlabbatupādānassa*], and they do not explain the full understanding of clinging to a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānassa*].
mn11:10.5: Because those good ascetics and brahmins do not truly understand these three grounds as they are.
mn11:10.6: Therefore, while claiming to speak of the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññāvādā*], those good ascetics and brahmins
mn11:10.7: do not correctly explain the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññaṁ*]—
mn11:10.8: they explain the full understanding of clinging to sensual pleasures [*kāmupādānassa*], but they do not explain the full understanding of clinging to views [*diṭṭhupādānassa*], they do not explain the full understanding of clinging to precepts and observances [*sīlabbatupādānassa*], and they do not explain the full understanding of clinging to a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānassa*].
mn11:11.1: There are, *bhikkhus*, some ascetics and brahmins who claim to speak of the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññāvādā*].
mn11:11.2: But they do not correctly explain the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññaṁ*]—
mn11:11.3: they explain the full understanding of clinging to sensual pleasures [*kāmupādānassa*], they explain the full understanding of clinging to views [*diṭṭhupādānassa*], but they do not explain the full understanding of clinging to precepts and observances [*sīlabbatupādānassa*], and they do not explain the full understanding of clinging to a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānassa*].
mn11:11.5: Because those good ascetics and brahmins do not truly understand these two grounds as they are.
mn11:11.6: Therefore, while claiming to speak of the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññāvādā*], those good ascetics and brahmins
mn11:11.7: do not correctly explain the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññaṁ*]—
mn11:11.8: they explain the full understanding of clinging to sensual pleasures [*kāmupādānassa*], they explain the full understanding of clinging to views [*diṭṭhupādānassa*], but they do not explain the full understanding of clinging to precepts and observances [*sīlabbatupādānassa*], and they do not explain the full understanding of clinging to a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānassa*].
mn11:12.1: There are, *bhikkhus*, some ascetics and brahmins who claim to speak of the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññāvādā*].
mn11:12.2: But they do not correctly explain the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññaṁ*]—
mn11:12.3: they explain the full understanding of clinging to sensual pleasures [*kāmupādānassa*], they explain the full understanding of clinging to views [*diṭṭhupādānassa*], they explain the full understanding of clinging to precepts and observances [*sīlabbatupādānassa*], but they do not explain the full understanding of clinging to a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānassa*].
mn11:12.5: Because those good ascetics and brahmins do not truly understand this one ground as it is.
mn11:12.6: Therefore, while claiming to speak of the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññāvādā*], those good ascetics and brahmins
mn11:12.7: do not correctly explain the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññaṁ*]—
mn11:12.8: they explain the full understanding of clinging to sensual pleasures [*kāmupādānassa*], they explain the full understanding of clinging to views [*diṭṭhupādānassa*], they explain the full understanding of clinging to precepts and observances [*sīlabbatupādānassa*], but they do not explain the full understanding of clinging to a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānassa*].
mn11:13.1: In such a *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*], *bhikkhus*, whatever confidence there is in the Teacher is not said to be well grounded;
mn11:13.2: whatever confidence there is in the *dhamma* is not said to be well grounded;
mn11:13.3: whatever bringing to fulfillment there is in ethical conduct is not said to be well grounded;
mn11:13.4: whatever dearness and agreeableness there is toward companions in the *dhamma* is not said to be well grounded.
mn11:13.6: For so it is, *bhikkhus*, in a *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*] that is badly explained, badly taught, not leading out, not conducive to peace, and not proclaimed by a perfectly awakened one.
mn11:14.1: But the Tathāgata, *bhikkhus*, the *arahant*, the perfectly awakened one, claiming to speak of the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññāvādo*], correctly explains the full understanding of all clinging [*sabbupādānapariññaṁ*]—
mn11:14.2: he explains the full understanding of clinging to sensual pleasures [*kāmupādānassa*], he explains the full understanding of clinging to views [*diṭṭhupādānassa*], he explains the full understanding of clinging to precepts and observances [*sīlabbatupādānassa*], and he explains the full understanding of clinging to a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānassa*].
mn11:15.1: In such a *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*], *bhikkhus*, whatever confidence there is in the Teacher is said to be well grounded;
mn11:15.2: whatever confidence there is in the *dhamma* is said to be well grounded;
mn11:15.3: whatever bringing to fulfillment there is in ethical conduct is said to be well grounded;
mn11:15.4: whatever dearness and agreeableness there is toward companions in the *dhamma* is said to be well grounded.
mn11:15.6: For so it is, *bhikkhus*, in a *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*] that is well explained, well taught, leading out, conducive to peace, and proclaimed by a perfectly awakened one.
mn11:16.1: And these four clingings [*upādānā*], *bhikkhus*—what is their source, what is their origin, from what are they born, from what do they come forth?
mn11:16.10: The dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanaṁ*] has *nāmarūpa* as its source, *nāmarūpa* as its origin, is born from *nāmarūpa*, and comes forth from *nāmarūpa*.
mn11:16.11: And this *nāmarūpa*, *bhikkhus*—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born, from what does it come forth?
mn11:16.12: *Nāmarūpa* has consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as its source, consciousness [*viññāṇa*] as its origin, is born from consciousness [*viññāṇa*], and comes forth from consciousness [*viññāṇa*].
mn11:16.13: And this consciousness [*viññāṇañcidaṁ*], *bhikkhus*—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born, from what does it come forth?
mn11:16.14: Consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] has *saṅkhāras* as its source, *saṅkhāras* as its origin, is born from *saṅkhāras*, and comes forth from *saṅkhāras*.
mn11:16.15: And these *saṅkhāras*, *bhikkhus*—what is their source, what is their origin, from what are they born, from what do they come forth?
mn11:16.16: *Saṅkhāras* have blindness [*avijjā*] as their source, blindness [*avijjā*] as their origin, are born from blindness [*avijjā*], and come forth from blindness [*avijjā*].
mn11:16.2: These four clingings [*upādānā*] have *taṇhā* as their source, *taṇhā* as their origin, are born from *taṇhā*, and come forth from *taṇhā*.
mn11:16.3: And this *taṇhā*, *bhikkhus*—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born, from what does it come forth?
mn11:16.4: *Taṇhā* has *vedanā* as its source, *vedanā* as its origin, is born from *vedanā*, and comes forth from *vedanā*.
mn11:16.5: And this *vedanā*, *bhikkhus*—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born, from what does it come forth?
mn11:16.6: *Vedanā* has contact as its source, contact as its origin, is born from contact, and comes forth from contact.
mn11:16.7: And this contact, *bhikkhus*—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born, from what does it come forth?
mn11:16.8: Contact has the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as its source, the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*] as its origin, is born from the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*], and comes forth from the dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatana*].
mn11:16.9: And this dimension of the six senses [*saḷāyatanañcidaṁ*], *bhikkhus*—what is its source, what is its origin, from what is it born, from what does it come forth?
mn11:17.1: And when, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* has abandoned blindness [*avijjā*] and knowledge has arisen, then, with the fading away of blindness [*avijjāvirāgā*] and the arising of knowledge, he does not cling to clinging to sensual pleasures [*kāmupādānaṁ*], he does not cling to clinging to views [*diṭṭhupādānaṁ*], he does not cling to clinging to precepts and observances [*sīlabbatupādānaṁ*], he does not cling to clinging to a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānaṁ*].
mn11:17.2: Not clinging, he does not tremble; not trembling, he personally attains final *nibbāna*.
mn11:17.3: He understands: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’”
mn11:17.5: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* rejoiced in the *Bhagavā*’s words.
mn11:17.6: The Shorter Lion’s Roar is finished, the first.
mn11:2.1: “Here alone, *bhikkhus*, is the ascetic; here the second ascetic, here the third ascetic, here the fourth ascetic;
mn11:2.2: other teachings are empty of ascetics.
mn11:2.3: In this way, *bhikkhus*, you should rightly roar the lion’s roar.
mn11:3.1: But, *bhikkhus*, it is possible that wanderers of other sects might say:
mn11:3.10: Friends, we have confidence in the Teacher, we have confidence in the *dhamma*, we bring ethical conduct to fulfillment;
mn11:3.11: and our companions in the *dhamma* are dear and agreeable—
mn11:3.12: both householders and those gone forth.
mn11:3.13: These, friends, are the four things declared to us by that *Bhagavā*, who knows and sees, who is an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened one, seeing which in ourselves we say—
mn11:3.14: “Here alone is the ascetic, here the second ascetic, here the third ascetic, here the fourth ascetic;
mn11:3.15: other teachings are empty of ascetics.”’
mn11:3.2: ‘But what assurance do the venerables have, what strength, by reason of which you venerables say—
mn11:3.3: “Here alone is the ascetic, here the second ascetic, here the third ascetic, here the fourth ascetic;
mn11:3.4: other teachings are empty of ascetics”?’
mn11:3.5: When wanderers of other sects speak thus, *bhikkhus*, they should be told:
mn11:3.6: ‘Friends, there are four things declared to us by that *Bhagavā*, who knows and sees, who is an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened one, seeing which in ourselves we say—
mn11:3.7: “Here alone is the ascetic, here the second ascetic, here the third ascetic, here the fourth ascetic;
mn11:3.8: other teachings are empty of ascetics.”
mn11:4.1: But, *bhikkhus*, it is possible that wanderers of other sects might say:
mn11:4.2: ‘Friends, we too have confidence in the Teacher who is our teacher, and we too have confidence in the *dhamma* that is our *dhamma*; we too bring to fulfillment the ethical practices that are ours.
mn11:4.3: Our companions in the *dhamma* too are dear and agreeable—
mn11:4.4: both householders and those gone forth.
mn11:4.5: So here, friends, what distinction is there, what superiority, what difference, between you and us?’
mn11:5.1: When wanderers of other sects speak thus, *bhikkhus*, they should be told:
mn11:5.10: ‘Friends, that goal is for one free of ill will, not for one with ill will.’
mn11:5.11: ‘But friends, is that goal for one with delusion, or for one free of delusion?’
mn11:5.12: Answering rightly, *bhikkhus*, the wanderers of other sects would answer thus:
mn11:5.13: ‘Friends, that goal is for one free of delusion, not for one with delusion.’
mn11:5.14: ‘But friends, is that goal for one with *taṇhā*, or for one free of *taṇhā* [*vītataṇhassa*]?’
mn11:5.15: Answering rightly, *bhikkhus*, the wanderers of other sects would answer thus:
mn11:5.16: ‘Friends, that goal is for one free of *taṇhā* [*vītataṇhassa*], not for one with *taṇhā*.’
mn11:5.17: ‘But friends, is that goal for one with clinging [*saupādānassa*], or for one without clinging [*anupādānassa*]?’
mn11:5.18: Answering rightly, *bhikkhus*, the wanderers of other sects would answer thus:
mn11:5.19: ‘Friends, that goal is for one without clinging [*anupādānassa*], not for one with clinging [*saupādānassa*].’
mn11:5.2: ‘But friends, is the goal one, or are the goals many?’
mn11:5.20: ‘But friends, is that goal for one who knows, or for one who does not know?’
mn11:5.21: Answering rightly, *bhikkhus*, the wanderers of other sects would answer thus:
mn11:5.22: ‘Friends, that goal is for one who knows, not for one who does not know.’
mn11:5.23: ‘But friends, is that goal for one who is attached and opposed, or for one who is neither attached nor opposed?’
mn11:5.24: Answering rightly, *bhikkhus*, the wanderers of other sects would answer thus:
mn11:5.25: ‘Friends, that goal is for one who is neither attached nor opposed, not for one who is attached and opposed.’
mn11:5.26: ‘But friends, is that goal for one who delights in proliferation and relishes proliferation, or for one who delights in non-proliferation and relishes non-proliferation?’
mn11:5.27: Answering rightly, *bhikkhus*, the wanderers of other sects would answer thus:
mn11:5.28: ‘Friends, that goal is for one who delights in non-proliferation and relishes non-proliferation, not for one who delights in proliferation and relishes proliferation.’
mn11:5.3: Answering rightly, *bhikkhus*, the wanderers of other sects would answer thus:
mn11:5.4: ‘Friends, the goal is one, not many goals.’
mn11:5.5: ‘But friends, is that goal for one with passion, or for one free of passion?’
mn11:5.6: Answering rightly, *bhikkhus*, the wanderers of other sects would answer thus:
mn11:5.7: ‘Friends, that goal is for one free of passion, not for one with passion.’
mn11:5.8: ‘But friends, is that goal for one with ill will, or for one free of ill will?’
mn11:5.9: Answering rightly, *bhikkhus*, the wanderers of other sects would answer thus:
mn11:6.2: the view of existence and the view of nonexistence.
mn11:6.3: For whatever ascetics or brahmins adhere to the view of existence, go over to the view of existence, and are seized by the view of existence, they are opposed to the view of nonexistence.
mn11:6.4: And whatever ascetics or brahmins adhere to the view of nonexistence, go over to the view of nonexistence, and are seized by the view of nonexistence, they are opposed to the view of existence.
mn11:7.1: And whatever ascetics or brahmins do not truly understand, as it is, the arising, the passing away, the gratification, the drawback, and the escape in regard to these two views, they are with passion, they are with ill will, they are with delusion, they are with *taṇhā*, they are with clinging [*saupādānā*], they do not know, they are attached and opposed, they delight in proliferation and relish proliferation;
mn11:7.2: they are not freed from birth, aging, and death, from sorrows, lamentations, pains, distresses, and despairs;
mn11:7.3: I say that they are not freed from *dukkha*.
mn11:8.1: But whatever ascetics or brahmins truly understand, as it is, the arising, the passing away, the gratification, the drawback, and the escape in regard to these two views, they are free of passion, free of ill will, free of delusion, free of *taṇhā* [*vītataṇhā*], without clinging [*anupādānā*], they know, they are neither attached nor opposed, they delight in non-proliferation and relish non-proliferation;
mn11:8.2: they are freed from birth, aging, and death, from sorrows, lamentations, pains, distresses, and despairs;
mn11:8.3: I say that they are freed from *dukkha*.
mn11:9.3: Clinging to sensual pleasures [*kāmupādānaṁ*], clinging to views [*diṭṭhupādānaṁ*], clinging to precepts and observances [*sīlabbatupādānaṁ*], and clinging to a doctrine of self [*attavādupādānaṁ*].
